retrieved_memories.content:
User is focused on reducing costs and managing risk in real estate transactions, particularly in rental purchases. They value clear, direct assessments of financing and risk trade-offs and use a hard-gate-by-phase approach to close deals. User prioritizes verifiable financials and requires concrete evidence before committing. User applies a clear rule: wait only if the delay offers real upside and doesn't increase exposure; push now if waiting adds weather, compliance, cost, or failure risk; and lock scope/pricing when uncertain. User now explicitly separates 'deal-killers' from 'renegotiation items' to guide pricing and closing decisions: deal-killers include bad title/unfixable lien, access problem, serious open permit that can’t be cured cleanly, income fraud, major water intrusion that’s been hidden; renegotiation items include stale repairs, weak rent collection, undocumented concessions, deferred maintenance, insurance hit only found through outside docs. User treats the absence of source documents as a material defect and will condition the LOI and PSA on timely document delivery and real diligence. User now recommends a revised, prioritized diligence sequence: 1) Verify the exact defect from the rejection note; 2) Pull the clean backup and the prior approval trail; 3) Pin responsibility on title / escrow / seller / lender; 4) Set a cure deadline; 5) Escalate if they miss it; 6) Resubmit once, clean; 7) Confirm acceptance in writing. User considers a defect a hard stop only if it threatens recording, funding, or priority. User’s core question for evaluating a property is now: 'Can this property close cleanly, and if yes, what does it really earn after outside verification?' User uses firm, concise messaging to terminate vendor negotiations when further back-and-forth is undesirable; this includes sending a short, direct message that explicitly refuses revised terms with no options or softening. When a vendor misses a deadline and requests another extension, User responds with a short, direct message that states the next step clearly and keeps the project moving. User now prefers to request explicit vendor confirmation that changes are accepted and whether the new version replaces prior terms, rather than asking for 'clean form' drafts as an admin task; User wants the tone to convey that they are in control and not waiting around. When presented with a proposal, User accepts it directly with a short affirmation (e.g., 'That works.') and does not request further explanation or negotiation. User is now also creating a structured handoff plan for messy projects with open items that need clear ownership to prevent loss of responsibility. User now enhances handoff templates by adding explicit 'Decision needed' and 'Escalation' columns and requires that every open item have a clear owner, due date, and proof requirement. User now recommends a minimal, stripped-down handoff template that includes only one owner, one due date, one proof item, and explicitly requires a clear escalation path. User states that the 'escalation column' is the part people hate but also the part that works because it enforces accountability. User prefers a tight, no-noodle handoff sheet that uses a concise header note: 'This sheet controls all open handoff items. If it has no owner, due date, decision needed, proof, or escalation path, it is not closed.' User intends to use this template on real projects and plans to strip it down further so that no one has room to delay or 'noodle around.' User is now actively managing stalled sales and needs practical, prioritized step orders to keep the process from drifting after a paperwork snag. New: User wants a tighter, more actionable sequence that explicitly controls the file and keeps leverage by specifying who controls the next move, what gets frozen, when to escalate, and what evidence is needed. User wants a sequence that starts with verifying the exact defect from the rejection note and includes pulling the clean backup and prior approval trail. User wants to pin responsibility on title / escrow / seller / lender. User sets a cure deadline and escalates if missed. User resubmits once, clean and confirms acceptance in writing. User needs to know how the sequence changes if the snag is recording-related versus funding-related. User now wants the actual call order by party (title vs escrow vs lender vs seller) in a clean sequence they can use without thinking. User now wants a working sequence with clear decision rules: call title first for the rejection reason and cure path; freeze the deal after title’s response; if title says it’s a lender condition, verify with lender and get the exact condition in writing; if escrow says it’s out of their hands, move to the next party (lender or seller as appropriate); if the snag is both recording and funding-related, prioritize recording issues and move to counsel. User accepts the proposed sequence as workable and practical and will use a mixed-defect rule: title first unless lender is clearly the only blocker. User will verify with the lender if title notes a 'lender condition' and will get the exact condition in writing. User will press the party that actually owns the defect (not escrow’s messenger version) if escrow says it’s out of their hands. User treats recording/priority-related defects as hard stops and will freeze only the defective piece while keeping the clean stack moving. User requires a deadline in writing and escalates the item the same day if the deadline is missed. User will use counsel when authority, recordability, or priority is in play. User now wants a tighter, more direct handoff and vendor communication that minimizes hand-holding and explicitly boxes in required answers. User prefers a direct request template that includes a named recipient, a deadline, an exact question, and a required answer format (yes/no/number/date/scope). User wants to reduce ambiguity by requiring explicit responses with no general updates or filler; the answer must be a direct 'yes/no', 'number', 'date', or 'scope' or must state exactly what’s missing and when the missing info will be available. User intends to use a sharp, stripped-down request template that includes the recipient's name, a deadline, the exact question, and a clear instruction to answer the question directly without general updates. User will use this template with their actual wording to force a direct, non-negotiable answer. User now uses a direct, boxed-in request template: 'Name, I need a direct answer by [time] to whether [action] can be finished by [date] or not. Answer yes/no. If not, give the exact date and remaining scope. If you can’t answer yet, say exactly what’s missing and when I’ll have it. No update. Just the answer.' User will remove the 'if you can’t answer yet' line to eliminate room for stalling. User has a firm policy of accepting proposals directly with a short affirmation and not requesting further explanation or negotiation; this is demonstrated by the message 'That’s it. Clean enough.' User requested tighter and firmer templates for vendor and handoff communications, asking to remove filler phrases like 'if you can’t answer yet.' User accepted a proposal directly with a short affirmation on 2023-07-15. New: User wants a practical, actionable decision grid for vendor responses that forces a yes/no call on the stuff that matters. User will use a specific, structured vendor response grid with columns: Vendor, Scope exactly matched?, Price actually moved?, Terms improved for me?, Exclusions/Carve-outs added?, Future change-order risk?, Keep/follow-up/out, Rank. User’s rule set is blunt: - Exact scope + no new exclusions + better terms = top - Exact scope + flat price + cleaner terms = still good - Lower price but added carve-outs or exclusions = below the above - Any vague "subject to" language or undefined allowances = follow-up at best - Scope dodge or scope rewrite = out unless the price is ridiculous and you can box them in hard. User now uses a one-line scoring row pasted under each vendor with a blunt rank rule and a tie-breaker for clean vs cheaper offers. User’s final rule is: 'The clean/enforceable one wins over the cheap sloppy one — every time, unless the price gap is big enough to justify the risk and I can box them in hard.' User defines the test for vendor offers as 'Exact scope + no exclusions + enforceable later.' User will use this test as the primary decision criterion for vendor offers. New: User wants the draft tightened to sound more like a hard stop, not a request; specifically wants to replace 'If this is a process issue' with a direct statement that requires the exact defect, the authority, and the cure deadline, and to state plainly if slowing down is procedural. User now uses a direct, boxed-in request template that requires the exact defect, the authority for the rejection, and the cure deadline, and will state plainly if slowing down is procedural. User intends to keep the direct request template exactly as drafted and only swap the bracketed fields when using it. User has a firm policy of accepting proposals directly with a short affirmation and not requesting further explanation or negotiation; this is demonstrated by the message 'That’s it. Clean enough.'

New: User now uses a direct, boxed-in request template that requires a redline showing every change to scope, cost, schedule, and warranty and states that current scope and terms stay in place until they review the redline. User will use a deadline to create leverage and keep the project moving. User will remove the 'if you can’t answer yet' line from the request template to eliminate room for stalling.

retrieved_memories.content:
User is focused on reducing costs and managing risk in real estate transactions, particularly in rental purchases. They value clear, direct assessments of financing and risk trade-offs and use a hard-gate-by-phase approach to close deals. User prioritizes verifiable financials and requires concrete evidence before committing. User applies a clear rule: wait only if the delay offers real upside and doesn't increase exposure; push now if waiting adds weather, compliance, cost, or failure risk; and lock scope/pricing when uncertain. User now explicitly separates 'deal-killers' from 'renegotiation items' to guide pricing and closing decisions: deal-killers include bad title/unfixable lien, access problem, serious open permit that can’t be cured cleanly, income fraud, major water intrusion that’s been hidden; renegotiation items include stale repairs, weak rent collection, undocumented concessions, deferred maintenance, insurance hit only found through outside docs. User treats the absence of source documents as a material defect and will condition the LOI and PSA on timely document delivery and real diligence. User now recommends a revised, prioritized diligence sequence: 1) Verify the exact defect from the rejection note; 2) Pull the clean backup and the prior approval trail; 3) Pin responsibility on title / escrow / seller / lender; 4) Set a cure deadline; 5) Escalate if they miss it; 6) Resubmit once, clean; 7) Confirm acceptance in writing. User considers a defect a hard stop only if it threatens recording, funding, or priority. User’s core question for evaluating a property is now: 'Can this property close cleanly, and if yes, what does it really earn after outside verification?' User uses firm, concise messaging to terminate vendor negotiations when further back-and-forth is undesirable; this includes sending a short, direct message that explicitly refuses revised terms with no options or softening. When a vendor misses a deadline and requests another extension, User responds with a short, direct message that states the next step clearly and keeps the project moving. User now prefers to request explicit vendor confirmation that changes are accepted and whether the new version replaces prior terms, rather than asking for 'clean form' drafts as an admin task; User wants the tone to convey that they are in control and not waiting around. When presented with a proposal, User accepts it directly with a short affirmation (e.g., 'That works.') and does not request further explanation or negotiation. User is now also creating a structured handoff plan for messy projects with open items that need clear ownership to prevent loss of responsibility. User now enhances handoff templates by adding explicit 'Decision needed' and 'Escalation' columns and requires that every open item have a clear owner, due date, and proof requirement. User now recommends a minimal, stripped-down handoff template that includes only one owner, one due date, one proof item, and explicitly requires a clear escalation path. User states that the 'escalation column' is the part people hate but also the part that works because it enforces accountability. User prefers a tight, no-noodle handoff sheet that uses a concise header note: 'This sheet controls all open handoff items. If it has no owner, due date, decision needed, proof, or escalation path, it is not closed.' User intends to use this template on real projects and plans to strip it down further so that no one has room to delay or 'noodle around.' User is now actively managing stalled sales and needs practical, prioritized step orders to keep the process from drifting after a paperwork snag. New: User wants a tighter, more actionable sequence that explicitly controls the file and keeps leverage by specifying who controls the next move, what gets frozen, when to escalate, and what evidence is needed. User wants a sequence that starts with verifying the exact defect from the rejection note and includes pulling the clean backup and prior approval trail. User wants to pin responsibility on title / escrow / seller / lender. User sets a cure deadline and escalates if missed. User resubmits once, clean and confirms acceptance in writing. User needs to know how the sequence changes if the snag is recording-related versus funding-related. User now wants the actual call order by party (title vs escrow vs lender vs seller) in a clean sequence they can use without thinking. User now wants a working sequence with clear decision rules: call title first for the rejection reason and cure path; freeze the deal after title’s response; if title says it’s a lender condition, verify with lender and get the exact condition in writing; if escrow says it’s out of their hands, move to the next party (lender or seller as appropriate); if the snag is both recording and funding-related, prioritize recording issues and move to counsel. User accepts the proposed sequence as workable and practical and will use a mixed-defect rule: title first unless lender is clearly the only blocker. User will verify with the lender if title notes a 'lender condition' and will get the exact condition in writing. User will press the party that actually owns the defect (not escrow’s messenger version) if escrow says it’s out of their hands. User treats recording/priority-related defects as hard stops and will freeze only the defective piece while keeping the clean stack moving. User requires a deadline in writing and escalates the item the same day if the deadline is missed. User will use counsel when authority, recordability, or priority is in play. User now wants a tighter, more direct handoff and vendor communication that minimizes hand-holding and explicitly boxes in required answers. User prefers a direct request template that includes a named recipient, a deadline, an exact question, and a required answer format (yes/no/number/date/scope). User wants to reduce ambiguity by requiring explicit responses with no general updates or filler; the answer must be a direct 'yes/no', 'number', 'date', or 'scope' or must state exactly what’s missing and when the missing info will be available. User intends to use a sharp, stripped-down request template that includes the recipient's name, a deadline, the exact question, and a clear instruction to answer the question directly without general updates. User will use this template with their actual wording to force a direct, non-negotiable answer. User now uses a direct, boxed-in request template: 'Name, I need a direct answer by [time] to whether [action] can be finished by [date] or not. Answer yes/no. If not, give the exact date and remaining scope. If you can’t answer yet, say exactly what’s missing and when I’ll have it. No update. Just the answer.' User will remove the 'if you can’t answer yet' line to eliminate room for stalling. User has a firm policy of accepting proposals directly with a short affirmation and not requesting further explanation or negotiation; this is demonstrated by the message 'That’s it. Clean enough.' User requested tighter and firmer templates for vendor and handoff communications, asking to remove filler phrases like 'if you can’t answer yet.' User accepted a proposal directly with a short affirmation on 2023-07-15. New: User wants a practical, actionable decision grid for vendor responses that forces a yes/no call on the stuff that matters. User will use a specific, structured vendor response grid with columns: Vendor, Scope exactly matched?, Price actually moved?, Terms improved for me?, Exclusions/Carve-outs added?, Future change-order risk?, Keep/follow-up/out, Rank. User’s rule set is blunt: - Exact scope + no new exclusions + better terms = top - Exact scope + flat price + cleaner terms = still good - Lower price but added carve-outs or exclusions = below the above - Any vague "subject to" language or undefined allowances = follow-up at best - Scope dodge or scope rewrite = out unless the price is ridiculous and you can box them in hard. User now uses a one-line scoring row pasted under each vendor with a blunt rank rule and a tie-breaker for clean vs cheaper offers. User’s final rule is: 'The clean/enforceable one wins over the cheap sloppy one — every time, unless the price gap is big enough to justify the risk and I can box them in hard.' User defines the test for vendor offers as 'Exact scope + no exclusions + enforceable later.' User will use this test as the primary decision criterion for vendor offers. New: User wants the draft tightened to sound more like a hard stop, not a request; specifically wants to replace 'If this is a process issue' with a direct statement that requires the exact defect, the authority, and the cure deadline, and to state plainly if slowing down is procedural. User now uses a direct, boxed-in request template that requires the exact defect, the authority for the rejection, and the cure deadline, and will state plainly if slowing down is procedural. User intends to keep the direct request template exactly as drafted and only swap the bracketed fields when using it. User has a firm policy of accepting proposals directly with a short affirmation and not requesting further explanation or negotiation; this is demonstrated by the message 'That’s it. Clean enough.'

New: User now uses a direct, boxed-in request template that requires a redline showing every change to scope, cost, schedule, and warranty and states that current scope and terms stay in place until they review the redline. User will use a deadline to create leverage and keep the project moving. User will remove the 'if you can’t answer yet' line from the request template to eliminate room for stalling.

User now recommends a revised, prioritized diligence sequence: 1) Verify the exact defect from the rejection note; 2) Pull the clean backup and the prior approval trail; 3) Pin responsibility on title / escrow / seller / lender; 4) Set a cure deadline; 5) Escalate if they miss it; 6) Resubmit once, clean; 7) Confirm acceptance in writing. User considers a defect a hard stop only if it threatens recording, funding, or priority. User’s core question for evaluating a property is now: 'Can this property close cleanly, and if yes, what does it really earn after outside verification?' User uses firm, concise messaging to terminate vendor negotiations when further back-and-forth is undesirable; this includes sending a short, direct message that explicitly refuses revised terms with no options or softening. When a vendor misses a deadline and requests another extension, User responds with a short, direct message that states the next step clearly and keeps the project moving. User now prefers to request explicit vendor confirmation that changes are accepted and whether the new version replaces prior terms, rather than asking for 'clean form' drafts as an admin task; User wants the tone to convey that they are in control and not waiting around. When presented with a proposal, User accepts it directly with a short affirmation (e.g., 'That works.') and does not request further explanation or negotiation. User is now also creating a structured handoff plan for messy projects with open items that need clear ownership to prevent loss of responsibility. User now enhances handoff templates by adding explicit 'Decision needed' and 'Escalation' columns and requires that every open item have a clear owner, due date, and proof requirement. User now recommends a minimal, stripped-down handoff template that includes only one owner, one due date, one proof item, and explicitly requires a clear escalation path. User states that the 'escalation column' is the part people hate but also the part that works because it enforces accountability. User prefers a tight, no-noodle handoff sheet that uses a concise header note: 'This sheet controls all open handoff items. If it has no owner, due date, decision needed, proof, or escalation path, it is not closed.' User intends to use this template on real projects and plans to strip it down further so that no one has room to delay or 'noodle around.' User is now actively managing stalled sales and needs practical, prioritized step orders to keep the process from drifting after a paperwork snag. New: User wants a tighter, more actionable sequence that explicitly controls the file and keeps leverage by specifying who controls the next move, what gets frozen, when to escalate, and what evidence is needed. User wants a sequence that starts with verifying the exact defect from the rejection note and includes pulling the clean backup and prior approval trail. User wants to pin responsibility on title / escrow / seller / lender. User sets a cure deadline and escalates if missed. User resubmits once, clean and confirms acceptance in writing. User needs to know how the sequence changes if the snag is recording-related versus funding-related. User now wants the actual call order by party (title vs escrow vs lender vs seller) in a clean sequence they can use without thinking. User now wants a working sequence with clear decision rules: call title first for the rejection reason and cure path; freeze the deal after title’s response; if title says it’s a lender condition, verify with lender and get the exact condition in writing; if escrow says it’s out of their hands, move to the next party (lender or seller as appropriate); if the snag is both recording and funding-related, prioritize recording issues and move to counsel. User accepts the proposed sequence as workable and practical and will use a mixed-defect rule: title first unless lender is clearly the only blocker. User will verify with the lender if title notes a 'lender condition' and will get the exact condition in writing. User will press the party that actually owns the defect (not escrow’s messenger version) if escrow says it’s out of their hands. User treats recording/priority-related defects as hard stops and will freeze only the defective piece while keeping the clean stack moving. User requires a deadline in writing and escalates the item the same day if the deadline is missed. User will use counsel when authority, recordability, or priority is in play. User now wants a tighter, more direct handoff and vendor communication that minimizes hand-holding and explicitly boxes in required answers. User prefers a direct request template that includes a named recipient, a deadline, an exact question, and a required answer format (yes/no/number/date/scope). User wants to reduce ambiguity by requiring explicit responses with no general updates or filler; the answer must be a direct 'yes/no', 'number', 'date', or 'scope' or must state exactly what’s missing and when the missing info will be available. User intends to use a sharp, stripped-down request template that includes the recipient's name, a deadline, the exact question, and a clear instruction to answer the question directly without general updates. User will use this template with their actual wording to force a direct, non-negotiable answer. User now uses a direct, boxed-in request template: 'Name, I need a direct answer by [time] to whether [action] can be finished by [date] or not. Answer yes/no. If not, give the exact date and remaining scope. If you can’t answer yet, say exactly what’s missing and when I’ll have it. No update. Just the answer.' User will remove the 'if you can’t answer yet' line to eliminate room for stalling. User has a firm policy of accepting proposals directly with a short affirmation and not requesting further explanation or negotiation; this is demonstrated by the message 'That’s it. Clean enough.' User requested tighter and firmer templates for vendor and handoff communications, asking to remove filler phrases like 'if you can’t answer yet.' User accepted a proposal directly with a short affirmation on 2023-07-15. New: User wants a practical, actionable decision grid for vendor responses that forces a yes/no call on the stuff that matters. User will use a specific, structured vendor response grid with columns: Vendor, Scope exactly matched?, Price actually moved?, Terms improved for me?, Exclusions/Carve-outs added?, Future change-order risk?, Keep/follow-up/out, Rank. User’s rule set is blunt: - Exact scope + no new exclusions + better terms = top - Exact scope + flat price + cleaner terms = still good - Lower price but added carve-outs or exclusions = below the above - Any vague "subject to" language or undefined allowances = follow-up at best - Scope dodge or scope rewrite = out unless the price is ridiculous and you can box them in hard. User now uses a one-line scoring row pasted under each vendor with a blunt rank rule and a tie-breaker for clean vs cheaper offers. User’s final rule is: 'The clean/enforceable one wins over the cheap sloppy one — every time, unless the price gap is big enough to justify the risk and I can box them in hard.' User defines the test for vendor offers as 'Exact scope + no exclusions + enforceable later.' User will use this test as the primary decision criterion for vendor offers. New: User wants the draft tightened to sound more like a hard stop, not a request; specifically wants to replace 'If this is a process issue' with a direct statement that requires the exact defect, the authority, and the cure deadline, and to state plainly if slowing down is procedural. User now uses a direct, boxed-in request template that requires the exact defect, the authority for the rejection, and the cure deadline, and will state plainly if slowing down is procedural. User intends to keep the direct request template exactly as drafted and only swap the bracketed fields when using it. User has a firm policy of accepting proposals directly with a short affirmation and not requesting further explanation or negotiation; this is demonstrated by the message 'That’s it. Clean enough.'

New: User now uses a direct, boxed-in request template that requires a redline showing every change to scope, cost, schedule, and warranty and states that current scope and terms stay in place until they review the redline. User will use a deadline to create leverage and keep the project moving. User will remove the 'if you can’t answer yet' line from the request template to eliminate room for stalling.

details.explanation: She appears to respect resistance when it is grounded in facts and protects outcomes. Empty disagreement would annoy her, but a hard correction that improves the result is likely welcome. She probably prefers challenge over passive agreement, as long as it is practical and not moralizing.
details.ground_truth: Accepts firm pushback if it is useful
details.predicted: low tolerance for pushback; wants direct, non-softened responses
details.slot_fill_reason: Notes say they want firm, concise, blunt messaging, explicitly refuse revised terms with no options, and want a clean direct rejection response with exact defect, authority, and cure deadline.
details.judge_reason: The prediction matches the idea that she accepts direct correction and prefers challenge, but it underplays that she tolerates pushback when it is useful rather than simply having low tolerance for it.

retrieved_memories.content: User prefers firm, concise messaging in vendor negotiations and explicitly refuses revised terms with no options or softening.
The user wants the tone to be blunt and assertive but not needy, emphasizing leverage without appearing aggressive.
User wants the tone to convey that they are in control and not waiting around.

User prefers to request a clean, direct response that includes the exact defect, the authority for the rejection, and the cure deadline, with a clear deadline for the response. User will respond to a rejection with a message that states the rejection is not actionable as written and requires the exact defect, the authority for the rejection, and the cure deadline. [Original note: 2026-04-28T22:52:57] Updated to reflect preference for a direct, boxed-in request template that demands the exact defect, the authority for the rejection, and the cure deadline, and will state plainly if slowing down is procedural. [New info: 2026-04-28T22:54:26]
User wants the messaging to be tighter and with more spine.
